job_summary: 3,200 sf commercial restaurant tenant improvement in Phoenix, AZ converting occupancy from B to A-2 assembly, with Type I kitchen hood, grease interceptor, ADA restroom and path-of-travel upgrades, sprinkler modifications, fire/life-safety review, and outdoor patio seating. This is a commercial interior alteration/change-of-occupancy job, so it triggers a Building Permit for the tenant improvement/change of use, plus companion trade permits/reviews for plumbing, mechanical, electrical, fire protection, and accessibility-related work as applicable.

permits_required.permit_type: Building Permit — Commercial Tenant Improvement / Change of Occupancy (B to A-2)
permits_required.portal_selection: Commercial Building Permit - Tenant Improvement / Change of Occupancy
permits_required.notes: Required for the occupancy change from Business (B) to Assembly (A-2), interior alterations, ADA restroom/path-of-travel upgrades, patio seating area review, and overall code compliance review.

permits_required.permit_type: Mechanical Permit — Commercial Kitchen Hood / Exhaust System
permits_required.notes: Required for the Type I kitchen hood, exhaust ductwork, make-up air coordination, and any associated mechanical equipment serving the restaurant kitchen.

permits_required.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — Grease Interceptor / Commercial Kitchen Plumbing
permits_required.notes: Required for the grease interceptor and any plumbing modifications tied to kitchen fixtures, waste lines, or interceptor sizing/installation.
Required for any new/reworked circuits, hood controls, kitchen equipment connections, lighting changes, emergency/egress lighting, and ADA-related electrical work.
Required for sprinkler head relocations, coverage changes, hydraulic recalculation, or any fire sprinkler system modifications caused by the tenant improvement.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Building Permit — Commercial Tenant Improvement / Change of Occupancy (B to A-2)
permits_required_logic.included_because: The scope includes a change of occupancy from B to A-2, which requires building code review, life-safety review, accessibility review, and tenant improvement approval.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Change of occupancy and commercial tenant improvement

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Mechanical Permit — Commercial Kitchen Hood / Exhaust System
permits_required_logic.included_because: A Type I kitchen hood is a commercial mechanical system and requires permit review for exhaust, make-up air, clearances, and equipment installation.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — Grease Interceptor / Commercial Kitchen Plumbing
permits_required_logic.included_because: Restaurant kitchen drainage and grease interceptor installation are plumbing scope and must be permitted.
Restaurant TI work typically includes electrical changes for kitchen equipment, hood controls, lighting, and life-safety systems.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Commercial tenant improvement with kitchen and ADA upgrades

permits_required_logic.included_because: Any change to the tenant layout, hood placement, or occupancy load can require sprinkler modifications and fire/life-safety review.

applying_office: City of Phoenix Planning & Development Department (PDD)
apply_address: 200 W. Washington Street, 2nd Floor, Phoenix, AZ 85003
fee_range: Fee Estimate: **$22,500-$41,000+** (structured floor — see breakdown). Components: ~$13,000 base permit + plan review (Phoenix, AZ commercial restaurant TI floor) × 1.0× jurisdiction multiplier + $3,500 change-of-occupancy adder + $5,500 hood-fire-suppression adder + $2,500 grease-interceptor adder + $4,000 fire-sprinkler-modify adder + $3,000 ada-path-of-travel adder. **Verify against current fee schedule before quoting.**

approval_timeline.simple: Over-the-counter only if the scope is very limited; for this restaurant TI expect plan submittal and review, not same-day issuance. Phoenix notes simple trade work can move in 1-3 days online, but this scope is not simple trade work.
approval_timeline.complex: About 28 days for initial plan review in Phoenix, and longer if fire/life-safety, accessibility, sprinkler, or occupancy-change corrections are needed. Express services may be available.

inspections.description: Inspector verifies framing/layout matches approved plans, rated corridor and tenant separations are maintained, ADA restroom clearances are correct, path-of-travel upgrades are installed, and any required fire-resistance assemblies are intact before concealment.
inspections.timing: Before drywall, ceiling closure, or finish work

inspections.description: Inspector checks Type I hood installation, duct routing, clearances to combustibles, grease duct construction, exhaust fan installation, make-up air coordination, and access for cleaning and service.
inspections.timing: Before hood enclosure and before final startup

inspections.description: Inspector verifies grease interceptor installation, drain slope, venting, fixture connections, cleanouts, and that kitchen waste is routed through the approved interceptor before concealment.
inspections.timing: Before slab patching, backfill, or wall/ceiling closure

inspections.description: Inspector checks new circuits, conductor sizing, panel capacity, equipment disconnects, hood controls, GFCI/AFCI where applicable, emergency lighting, and proper labeling before walls and ceilings are closed.
inspections.timing: Before insulation, drywall, or ceiling closure

inspections.description: Inspector or fire protection reviewer checks sprinkler head spacing, coverage changes, hydraulic calculations if required, alarm interface impacts, and that the final layout does not block required fire protection coverage.
inspections.timing: Before final ceiling closure and before occupancy

inspections.description: Inspector verifies all approved work is complete, ADA path of travel is usable, restroom fixtures and clearances comply, hood and life-safety systems are operational, and the space is ready for occupancy as A-2 assembly.
inspections.timing: After all trade finals and before opening to the public

inspection_booking: Schedule inspections through the Phoenix Accela portal: https://aca-prod.accela.com/PHOENIX. Phoenix requires a minimum 24-hour advance notice; same-day requests are not accepted. Use the permit record to request each trade inspection separately if needed. Phone: (602) 262-7811. Plan on standard business hours for the Development Center; confirm current inspection cutoff times in the portal before submitting.
license_required: Arizona ROC-licensed contractor pulls the permit on behalf of the owner or tenant. The contractor's ROC license number must be on the application. For this commercial restaurant TI, the general contractor typically pulls the building permit, and licensed trade contractors pull their respective trade permits or are listed on the combined permit package as required by Phoenix.
city_contractor_registration: Phoenix requires a separate City Contractor Registration in addition to the Arizona ROC license. If the contractor is not currently registered with the City of Phoenix, the permit application can be rejected or blocked. Verify registration status and renewal before submitting any permit application.
what_to_bring: Completed Phoenix Accela permit application and contractor registration information
Arizona ROC license number, expiration date, and city contractor registration proof
Full plan set for the tenant improvement, including life-safety, occupancy, ADA, and reflected ceiling plans
Kitchen equipment cut sheets for the Type I hood, exhaust fan, make-up air unit, and any cooking appliances
Plumbing plans and grease interceptor sizing/details
Sprinkler/fire protection drawings and any required hydraulic calculations
Owner/tenant authorization letter and project valuation
common_mistakes: Submitting the application without current City of Phoenix contractor registration — an expired or lapsed city registration will block the permit even if the ROC license is valid.
Treating the occupancy change like a simple tenant finish-out — B to A-2 triggers life-safety, occupancy load, and accessibility review.
Forgetting that the Type I hood usually triggers separate mechanical review for exhaust, make-up air, and clearances.
Installing the grease interceptor without approved plumbing sizing and routing — inspectors will fail the rough-in if the interceptor is undersized or bypassed.
Assuming sprinkler changes are covered by the building permit — fire protection modifications need their own review/permit path.
Opening the patio seating area without confirming occupancy load, egress, and any required patio/fire separation details on the approved plans.
pro_tips: Check your Phoenix contractor registration renewal date before starting the application — registration lapses are one of the most common avoidable rejections.

Submit the full coordinated plan set at once: building, mechanical, plumbing, electrical, and fire protection. Phoenix plan review goes faster when the hood, grease interceptor, and sprinkler sheets match the architectural layout.
Have the kitchen equipment schedule and hood submittal ready before plan review; missing cut sheets are a common delay on restaurant TI jobs.
If the patio seating is part of the occupancy count, get that clarified in the first submittal so you do not have to revise occupant load and egress later.
Use the Accela portal to track comments and resubmittals; Phoenix is much faster when corrections are uploaded in one complete response instead of piecemeal.

code_citation.text: Any owner or authorized agent who intends to construct, enlarge, alter, repair, move, demolish or change the occupancy of a building or structure shall first make application to the building official and obtain the required permit. Occupancy classification shall be determined in accordance with Chapter 3.

companion_permits.permit_type: Fire Alarm Permit — Commercial Fire/Life-Safety Modifications
companion_permits.reason: May be required if: Fire alarm devices, panel, monitoring, or notification layout changes are part of the scope.
companion_permits.required_if: Fire alarm devices, panel, monitoring, or notification layout changes are part of the scope
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

companion_permits.reason: May be required if: Any portion of the patio occupies or affects public ROW.
companion_permits.required_if: Any portion of the patio occupies or affects public ROW
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

zoning_hoa_flag: This is a commercial restaurant project, so HOA restrictions usually do not apply. Zoning and site plan issues may still apply for outdoor patio seating, including use approval, occupant load, parking, accessibility, and any encroachment into the public right-of-way. If the building is in a historic district or special overlay area, additional design review may be required.
disclaimer: Data sourced Apr 28, 2026. Always verify current requirements directly with your local building department before starting work. Permit fees and requirements change frequently.
confidence_reason: Verified city/trade data found from official sources. Needs review for: fee_range

_meta.job_type: 3,200 sf commercial restaurant tenant improvement: change of occupancy B to A-2 assembly, Type I kitchen hood, grease interceptor, ADA restroom and path of travel upgrades, sprinkler modifications, fire/life-safety review, outdoor patio seating

fee_source.title: Fees, Valuations and Assurances | City of Phoenix

ahj_contact_source.title: Residential Buildings and Permits | City of Phoenix

code_section_source.title: [PDF] Plan Review, Permit, Inspection Criteria for Energy Systems

checklist: Verify your contractor license is current and valid in AZ
Confirm your liability insurance is active (certificate may be required at permit office)
Have the property address and legal parcel number ready
Know the project value/cost — required on most permit applications
Type I commercial exhaust hood listed/labeled per UL 710 with NFPA 96 compliance and AHJ-approved capture velocity per IMC 507
ANSUL R-102 (or equivalent UL 300) hood suppression: post-install discharge test certificate + every-6-month cleaning + annual inspection per NFPA 96 §11
ANSUL discharge auto-shutoffs all hood gas + electric fuel sources per IMC 507.7 — verify with AHJ during witness test
Grease interceptor sized per IPC 1003.3.4 by drainage fixture units (4-comp + mop typically 50+ GPM hydromechanical OR 750–1,500 gal exterior gravity); located ≤25 ft developed length from fixtures
Makeup air system balanced to ±10% of hood exhaust per IMC 506.3.4; tempered if > 35°F differential to prevent backdraft
Indirect waste with air gap on dishwasher, ice maker, prep sink, and steam kettle per IPC 802; commercial dishwasher backflow prevention (RPZ) per IPC 608.13.2
Dedicated mop sink with hose bib in janitor closet (IBC 902.3 / local health code) — required before health dept sign-off
ADA fixture counts per IBC Table 2902.1 + 60 in turning radius + lavatory pipe wrap insulation per ADA §606.5 + mirror reflective edge ≤40 in AFF per ADA §603.3
Local health department food establishment plan review + final inspection sign-off REQUIRED before Certificate of Occupancy (parallel filing — file early)
TABC / state liquor authority clearance if alcohol service — separate filing from building permit
Walk-in cooler envelope per IECC 403.10.1 (≥R-25 medium-temp panels, ≥R-32 freezer panels); door gasket seal verification at final
Existing sprinkler system reuse: hydraulic recalc for new layout + current 5-year cert per NFPA 25; new heads in cooking equipment area must be K-25+ per NFPA 13
Floor drains within 5 ft of kitchen equipment per IPC 411 with backwater valves where required
Fire alarm + emergency lighting per NFPA 72/101 with current battery capacity test on file
Change of occupancy memo (B/M → A-2): include occupant load + exits + restroom count + sprinkler/fire-alarm impacts per IEBC §1001.2/1001.3 + IBC §303.3 (Group A-2)

Maricopa County (or local equivalent) Environmental Services Department food-establishment plan review + final inspection sign-off REQUIRED before opening — coordinate with City CO schedule (MCESD: maricopa.gov)

Backflow prevention on EVERY food-service water connection: carbonated beverage (ASSE 1022 dual check), chemical dispenser at mop/service sinks, hose bibbs, dish-machine/ice/RO/espresso, pre-rinse — testable assemblies certified annually
Outdoor patio / sidewalk dining: verify city zoning (use permit may apply, e.g. Phoenix ZO §1207); include patio in occupant load + exiting + accessible route + sprinkler/awning review

Phoenix-specific (or equivalent local rule): contractor must be AZ ROC licensed AND associated with the Phoenix SHAPE PHX permit account before submittal (A.R.S. Title 32 Ch. 10) — frequently-missed pre-submittal blocker

Realistic fee warning: a 3,200 sq ft restaurant TI in Phoenix typically lands $8K–$25K+ across plan review + permit + fire + utility + health + zoning. Reject token min-fee outputs; calculate from the live fee schedule before quoting
Occupancy reclassification: identify existing approved occupancy + proposed group; require code-official approval + new/updated Certificate of Occupancy (IEBC §§1001.1, 1001.2, 1001.3; IBC Ch. 3)
Height/area limit recheck: compare new occupancy hazard category to existing construction type, allowable area, frontage/sprinkler increases, fire-wall strategy (IEBC §1011.5; IBC Ch. 5)

Fire-resistance + separation reassessment: re-verify fire barriers, horizontal assemblies, exterior wall ratings/openings, shaft enclosures, mixed-occupancy separations for the new hazard category (IEBC §§1011.1, 1011.5.3, 1011.6/7; IBC Ch. 7)
Fire protection re-run: sprinkler, fire alarm, hood suppression, and monitoring requirements at the new occupancy + IBC/IFC Ch. 9 thresholds (IEBC §§1004.1, 1011.2; IFC Ch. 9)

Means of egress recalculation: occupant load + number of exits + egress capacity + travel distance + common path + dead ends + door hardware updated to new occupancy (IEBC §§1005.1, 1011.4; IBC Ch. 10)
Accessibility / path-of-travel trigger: alterations to primary-function areas trigger ADA upgrades to altered area + serving amenities (2010 ADA §202.4 + 28 CFR §36.403)
Mechanical ventilation/exhaust recheck against the new occupancy: IMC ventilation, commercial-kitchen exhaust + makeup air, hazardous exhaust, energy compliance (IEBC §1008.1; IMC Ch. 4-6 + §507)
Plumbing demand + fixture-count recheck: water supply, sanitary load, fixture count, food-handling waste, grease/oil interceptors, chemical-waste approval (IEBC §§1009.1-1009.4; IPC Ch. 4 + 10)

Identify altered primary-function area: if scope alters an area where major activity occurs, an accessible path of travel to the altered area is required unless technically infeasible / disproportionate (2010 ADA §202.4; 28 CFR §36.403(a))
20% disproportionality cap: ADA path-of-travel work exceeding 20% of alteration cost is disproportionate at the federal level, but lower-cost items must still be prioritized (28 CFR §36.403(f)(1))

Accessible exterior route: site arrival points + accessible parking/passenger loading + curb ramps + walking surfaces + ramps/landings + slope/cross-slope + door clearances to altered area (2010 ADA §§206.2.1, 206.4, 403, 404, 405, 406)

Restroom path + restroom upgrades: if restrooms serve the altered area, accessible route + door + turning space + lavatory + water closet + grab bars + clearances + signage (2010 ADA §§213, 603-606, 609, 703)
Parking + passenger loading: recalculate accessible-stall count + van space + access aisle + signage for affected parking serving the altered area (2010 ADA §§208, 502, 503; 28 CFR §36.403(e)(1))
Drinking fountains, phones, and signage along the path: tactile/visual signs, room ID, directional, fountain units, public phones if any (2010 ADA §§211, 216, 602, 703, 704)
Doors, thresholds, and hardware on the route: clear width + maneuvering clearance + opening force + thresholds + lever/operable hardware + protruding objects (2010 ADA §§303, 307, 309, 404)

Confirm the required license is active and matches the application: Arizona ROC-licensed contractor pulls the permit on behalf of the owner or tenant. The contractor's ROC license number must be on the application. For this commercial restaurant TI, the general contractor typically pulls the building permit, and licensed trade contractors pull their respective trade permits or are listed on the combined permit package as required by Phoenix.
Bring 2 copies of all documents to City of Phoenix Planning & Development Department (PDD)

rejection_patterns.pattern: Missing Phoenix City Contractor Registration (state ROC not sufficient)

rejection_patterns.fix: Schedule inspections minimum 24 hours in advance through city portal

fee_calculator.note: Provide job_value to calculate an exact fee where formulas are available.

expert_notes.title: Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) — residential vs commercial vs dual licensing
expert_notes.note: Arizona is unusual in that the ROC issues SEPARATE license classifications for residential, commercial, and dual (both) work — a contractor licensed only for commercial work cannot legally pull a residential permit and vice versa. Verify the license class matches the project type (e.g., B-General Residential Contractor, CR-class residential trade licenses, KB-General Commercial) before contracting; a mismatched class voids lien rights and exposes the contractor to ROC discipline. Check status at the ROC Customer Portal before signing.
expert_notes.applies_to: All licensed construction work in Arizona

expert_notes.title: $1,000 unlicensed-work threshold (handyman exemption)
expert_notes.note: Arizona requires an ROC license for any single project where the combined value of labor and materials exceeds $1,000, OR that requires a building permit regardless of value. Handyman work under $1,000 with no permit required is exempt, but bundling small jobs to stay under the cap is treated as unlicensed contracting. HVAC, electrical, and plumbing work above $1,000 specifically requires the matching trade classification (e.g., CR-39 Air Conditioning & Refrigeration, CR-11 Electrical, CR-37 Plumbing).
expert_notes.applies_to: All paid construction work in Arizona ≥ $1,000 or requiring a permit

expert_notes.title: ARS § 9-835 / § 9-836 municipal permit shot clocks
expert_notes.note: Arizona Revised Statutes 9-835 (administrative completeness) and 9-836 (substantive review) require every municipality to publish licensing time-frames and to complete review within them — typically 30 working days for administrative completeness review and a published substantive review window for residential building permits. Missed deadlines entitle the applicant to a refund of a percentage of fees and can be escalated. Always demand the AHJ's published time-frame document and track the clock from the date the application is deemed administratively complete.
expert_notes.applies_to: All municipal building permit applications in Arizona

expert_notes.title: New 15-day residential plan review requirement
expert_notes.note: A new Arizona state law shortens the substantive review timeline for certain residential permits to 15 working days, accelerating the prior ARS 9-836 framework. AHJs are responding with expedited-review tracks, model-plan libraries, and staff capacity additions. For straightforward SFR/ADU applications using model plans or master-plan certifications, push for the 15-day track and flag any AHJ that quotes a longer turnaround as out of statutory compliance.
expert_notes.applies_to: Residential building permits in incorporated Arizona municipalities

expert_notes.title: No statewide building code — 106 separate AHJs
expert_notes.note: Arizona does NOT adopt a statewide building or residential code. Instead, all 15 counties and every incorporated municipality act as independent authorities having jurisdiction (AHJs), producing roughly 106 building-regulating jurisdictions statewide, each adopting their own edition of the IBC/IRC/IECC with local amendments. Always confirm the exact code edition and local amendments for the parcel's jurisdiction before drawing — Phoenix, Tucson, Mesa, and unincorporated Maricopa County can be on different code cycles simultaneously.
expert_notes.applies_to: All Arizona construction — code edition varies by jurisdiction

expert_notes.title: Phoenix 2024 PBCC adoption
expert_notes.note: On June 18, 2025, the Phoenix City Council adopted the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code (PBCC), which is based on the 2024 ICC family of codes including the 2024 IECC for energy. Projects in Phoenix submitted after the effective date must use the 2024 PBCC; projects in adjacent jurisdictions (Scottsdale, Tempe, Glendale, unincorporated Maricopa County) may still be on 2018 or 2021 cycles. Confirm the applicable PBCC edition with Phoenix PDD before plan-set finalization.

expert_notes.title: Tucson / Pima County 2024 code adoption effective Jan 1, 2026
expert_notes.note: Tucson and Pima County jointly adopted updated building codes (2024 IBC/IRC/IECC family with local amendments) that took effect January 1, 2026, after a joint recommendation from the City Manager and Pima County administrators. Permits submitted on or after that date are reviewed under the new codes; applications in process before the cutoff may be grandfathered at the AHJ's discretion. Always confirm which edition applies on the specific submittal date.
expert_notes.applies_to: City of Tucson and Pima County permit applications crossing 2026-01-01

expert_notes.title: Active Management Area (AMA) 100-year Assured Water Supply
expert_notes.note: Arizona law requires residential developers in designated groundwater Active Management Areas (Phoenix, Tucson, Pinal, Prescott, Santa Cruz) to obtain a Certificate of Assured Water Supply (CAWS) proving a 100-year water supply before plats can be approved or building permits issued for new subdivisions. ADWR has been under a moratorium on new groundwater-based CAWS in the Phoenix AMA since June 2023, and a 2026 court ruling further unsettled the rule — confirm CAWS status and whether the project sits inside an AMA before promising a build schedule.
expert_notes.applies_to: New residential subdivisions inside designated AMAs

expert_notes.title: ARS § 11-321 — county building permits and utility preemption
expert_notes.note: ARS 11-321 governs county-issued building permits and explicitly preserves utility providers' separate authority to operate, meaning a county building permit does NOT authorize utility connection or interconnection on its own. The contractor must file a parallel utility application (service connection, meter set, or DG interconnection) with the serving utility — county permit issuance does not waive that step. Always run the county building permit and the utility filing in parallel to avoid a finished build that cannot be energized.

expert_notes.title: APS distributed-generation interconnection — separate filing from building permit
expert_notes.note: Solar PV and battery systems in APS territory require a separate Interconnection Application filed with APS in addition to the AHJ building/electrical permit. APS may request a copy of the AHJ building permit before issuing Permission to Operate (PTO), and the system cannot legally export until PTO is granted. File the APS interconnection package as soon as the design is finalized — do not wait for the building permit, since the two reviews run in parallel and APS turnaround often controls the project's energization date.
expert_notes.applies_to: Residential solar PV and battery storage in APS service territory

expert_notes.title: HB 2301 / Mesa solar permit pre-interconnection requirement
expert_notes.note: HB 2301 reshaped Arizona's solar permitting landscape effective 2026, and for Mesa Electric Utility customers specifically, the interconnection application must be received and accepted BEFORE the system is installed (not after as in most jurisdictions). Installing first and applying after is grounds for denial of interconnection. Verify the serving utility's sequencing rule (APS, SRP, TEP, Mesa, and municipal utilities each have their own) before scheduling installation.
expert_notes.applies_to: Residential solar PV in Mesa Electric Utility territory

expert_notes.title: Owner-builder affidavit required on every permit (ARS 11-1605)
expert_notes.note: Per ARS 11-1605, every Arizona building permit requires written documentation of either an Arizona-licensed contractor pulling the permit OR an owner-builder classification signed by the property owner. Owner-builder permits restrict resale within one year (the owner must occupy and cannot have built for sale) and shift all liability to the owner. Counties (e.g., Maricopa) will not accept a permit application without this signed contractor/owner-builder declaration — prepare it before counter intake.

expert_notes.title: Rezoning shot clock — separate from permit shot clock
expert_notes.note: A new Arizona development 'shot clock' law imposes statutory time limits on rezoning decisions, distinct from the ARS 9-835/9-836 permit clocks. Local ordinances (e.g., Prescott Valley 13-14-070) implement parallel administrative-completeness and substantive-review windows for rezoning applications. If a project requires rezoning before permitting, treat it as TWO sequential clocks (rezoning, then permit) — and document each completeness determination in writing to preserve refund/escalation rights.
expert_notes.applies_to: Projects requiring rezoning prior to building permit

expert_notes.title: ROC license filing address and portal
expert_notes.note: ROC license applications, renewals, and complaints are filed via the ROC Customer Portal online, or by mail to Registrar of Contractors, P.O. Box 6748, Phoenix, AZ 85005-6748, or in person at 1700 West Washington Street, Suite 105, Phoenix, AZ 85007-2812. Use the portal for fastest turnaround and to pull license ID cards on demand. Expired or suspended licenses block permit issuance at most AHJ counters — verify status the day of submittal.

hidden_triggers.title: B/M-to-A-2 restaurant conversion can trigger sprinkler retrofit review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: A dine-in restaurant often changes the occupancy classification and fire/life-safety basis. In Phoenix, change-of-occupancy sprinkler triggers can be the budget item that surprises the tenant.
likely_required_actions: Identify existing and proposed occupancy groups
Run sprinkler applicability and tenant-area/building-area thresholds
Add fire sprinkler notes or separate sprinkler deferred submittal
companion_permits: Fire sprinkler permit, if retrofit/relocation is required
citations: IEBC Chapter 10 [verify adopted edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Change of occupancy (B/S to A-2/M) can trigger sprinkler retrofit and IEBC review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Converting an office, warehouse, or retail tenant to assembly or mercantile crosses occupancy classifications. The new occupant load can cross IBC 903.2 sprinkler thresholds and require an IEBC change-of-occupancy analysis even when the construction work looks small.
likely_required_actions: Run IEBC change-of-occupancy analysis (Chapter 10)
Recompute occupant load and check IBC 903.2 sprinkler thresholds
Update accessible route and restroom/fixture counts to the higher occupancy
Coordinate fire-alarm and egress lighting for the proposed occupancy
companion_permits: Fire sprinkler permit if retrofit/relocation triggered
Fire alarm permit if devices/coverage change
citations: IEBC Chapter 10 [verify adopted edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Grease interceptor / FOG review may be required for commercial food prep
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: FOG requirements are often enforced by sewer/environmental agencies outside the normal building-permit checklist. Missing sizing or location approval can force plumbing redesign.
likely_required_actions: Size interceptor from fixture/equipment schedule
Confirm location/access with sewer or environmental authority
Show sampling port/cleanout details if required
companion_permits: FOG / industrial waste / sewer approval, if required

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant seating changes require occupant-load and egress recalculation
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Adding dining, bar, queueing, or patio seats can change the required number of exits, door swing, panic hardware, travel distance, restroom counts, and fire-alarm assumptions.
likely_required_actions: Add occupant-load table by room/area
Show common path, exit access travel distance, and exit count
Coordinate exit signage/emergency lighting

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant work may need county food-establishment plan review before CO
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Health-department approval often runs parallel to building review. Missing it can block opening even when the building permit is ready for final.
likely_required_actions: Submit food-establishment plans and equipment schedule
Show finish schedule, hand sinks, mop sink, warewashing, and refrigeration
Coordinate health inspection before final CO
citations: Maricopa County Environmental Health Code [verify before merging]
FDA Food Code as adopted by local health authority [verify before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant work needs local health-department food-establishment plan review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Health-department review usually runs in parallel with the building permit and approves finish schedule, hand sinks, three-compartment sink, warewashing, refrigeration, and food-flow. Missing it can hold the certificate of occupancy even when building review is finished.
likely_required_actions: Submit food-establishment plans and equipment schedule to local health department
Show finish schedule, hand sinks, mop sink, three-compartment / warewashing, refrigeration
Coordinate health inspection before final CO
citations: FDA Food Code as adopted by local health authority [verify edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Restroom alteration can trigger ADA path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Altering a primary-function area or restroom can require upgrades to the accessible route, restrooms, drinking fountains, telephones, and parking up to the 20% disproportionality cap.
likely_required_actions: Prepare ADA path-of-travel cost allocation
Show accessible route to altered area
Verify restroom clearances, accessories, lavatory, mirror, and door hardware

hidden_triggers.title: Commercial alteration can require accessible-route/path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Even when the requested work is not 'an ADA project,' altered primary-function areas can require accessible route, restroom, drinking fountain, parking, and entrance upgrades within the disproportionality cap.
likely_required_actions: Prepare path-of-travel analysis
Budget required accessible upgrades
Document 20% disproportionality cap if used

hidden_triggers.title: Occupancy or occupant-load change can alter required restroom fixture counts
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: A retail/office/assembly change may look like tenant buildout but can require additional fixtures or accessible upgrades if occupant load or use category changes.
likely_required_actions: Prepare occupant-load and plumbing-fixture-count table
Check accessible fixture requirements
Coordinate any restroom expansion with path-of-travel obligations

hidden_triggers.title: Outdoor patio seating can trigger zoning, occupant-load, and canopy/fire review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Patios often look like a minor furniture change but can affect land-use permissions, exits, plumbing fixture counts, accessible route, fire access, canopy construction, and sprinkler coverage.
likely_required_actions: Confirm zoning/use permit or outdoor dining approval
Include patio seats in occupant-load/restroom counts
Review canopy/sprinkler coverage and fire access
companion_permits: Zoning/use permit or outdoor dining approval, if required

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant occupancy or suppression work may require fire-alarm interface review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Suppression systems, duct detectors, occupant load, and sprinkler changes can all require alarm interlocks, monitoring updates, or a separate fire-alarm submittal.
likely_required_actions: Confirm whether alarm shop drawings are deferred or separate
Show suppression/alarm interface sequence
Update monitoring account and acceptance testing
companion_permits: Fire alarm permit, if devices/interface change

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant seat count can change required plumbing fixtures
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Dining, bar, patio, and assembly occupant loads can push the project over restroom fixture thresholds, affecting cost, space planning, and accessibility upgrades.
likely_required_actions: Prepare occupant-load and fixture-count table
Verify male/female or all-gender fixture distribution rules
Coordinate accessible restroom clearances

_fee_floor_components.scope_note: plan review + permit + fire + utility + health
_fee_floor_components.jurisdiction_rationale: baseline calibration from Phoenix restaurant TI test case [verify current Phoenix Planning & Development fees]
